name: Roman Expanded
description: Comprehensive expansion of Roman mythology with additional heroes, deities, and mythological beings.

beings.name: Aeneas
beings.summary: Trojan hero who fled fallen Troy and founded Rome through divine destiny. Aeneas escaped Troy with his father and son, wandering the Mediterranean under divine guidance before reaching Italy. He established the foundation that would become Rome, fulfilling Jupiter's prophecy. Represents the Roman ideal of divine destiny and the founding myth that connects Rome to heroic Trojan past.

beings.name: Romulus
beings.summary: Founder and first king of Rome who killed his twin brother Remus. Romulus and Remus were sons of Mars, raised by a she-wolf before founding Rome. Romulus killed Remus in a dispute over the city's boundaries, establishing the pattern of Roman fraternal conflict and the cost of foundation.

beings.name: Remus
beings.summary: Co-founder of Rome killed by his twin brother Romulus over city boundaries. Remus and Romulus were twin sons of Mars who disagreed on where to build Rome. Remus mocked the city walls by jumping over them, leading Romulus to kill him in rage. Represents the tragic cost of urban foundation and Roman acceptance of violence in state-building.

beings.name: Juno
beings.summary: Queen of the gods and protector of marriage who constantly punished Jupiter's lovers. Juno presides over childbirth and marriage while fiercely protecting her divine marriage from Jupiter's infidelities. She relentlessly pursues Jupiter's mortal lovers and their children. Represents the Roman ideal of marital fidelity and the destructive power of jealousy in divine relationships.

beings.name: Minerva
beings.summary: Goddess of wisdom, crafts, and strategic warfare who sprang fully armed from Jupiter's head. Minerva represents intellectual warfare and civilized arts, unlike Mars' brutal warfare. She invented many crafts and protects cities with strategic wisdom. Embodies the Roman values of intellectual achievement and strategic thinking in both war and peace.

beings.name: Diana
beings.summary: Goddess of the hunt, moon, and childbirth who protects young women and wild nature. Diana roams forests with her nymph followers, hunting with silver bow and arrows while protecting the wilderness and its creatures. She fiercely punishes those who violate her sacred spaces or spy on her bathing. Represents the Roman appreciation for wild nature and the protection of female independence.

beings.name: Vulcan
beings.summary: God of fire and metalworking who creates divine weapons and tools in his underground forge. Vulcan was cast from heaven and became lame, perfecting his craft in underground workshops. He forged Jupiter's thunderbolts and created marvelous automata for his workshops. Represents the Roman mastery of technology and the power of craftsmanship to overcome physical limitations.

beings.name: Venus
beings.summary: Goddess of love and beauty whose influence extends to politics and warfare. Venus governs romantic love, sexual desire, and political harmony. She was mother of Aeneas and guided his founding of Rome. Her power could make enemies fall in love and cities surrender peacefully. Represents the Roman understanding of love as both personal and political force.

beings.name: Bacchus
beings.summary: God of wine, ecstasy, and theater who liberates followers through intoxication. Bacchus travels the world teaching viticulture and leading wild processions of ecstatic worship. His festivals break social constraints and allow temporary freedom from Roman strictness. Represents the Roman need for controlled release and the power of wine to transcend social boundaries.

beings.name: Ceres
beings.summary: Goddess of agriculture and grain who created seasons through her grief. Ceres' daughter Proserpina was abducted by Pluto, causing Ceres to neglect the earth and create winter. When Proserpina returns part-time, Ceres brings spring and summer. Represents the Roman connection between divine emotion and natural cycles.

beings.name: Pluto
beings.summary: God of the underworld who abducted Proserpina to be his queen. Pluto ruled the realm of the dead and desired a wife, abducting Ceres' daughter Proserpina. This created the seasonal cycle as Ceres mourned. Represents Roman understanding of death as ruler of a kingdom rather than evil force.

beings.name: Proserpina
beings.summary: Queen of the underworld whose seasonal return creates spring and summer. Proserpina was abducted by Pluto while gathering flowers, becoming his underworld queen. Jupiter decreed she spend part of year with her mother Ceres, creating the seasons. Represents the Roman connection between death and renewal.

beings.name: Faunus
beings.summary: God of wild forests and prophecy who appears as half-goat, half-man. Faunus inhabits the deep woods, sometimes frightening travelers but also giving prophecies to those who seek his wisdom. He represents the untamed aspects of nature and the mysterious knowledge found in wilderness.

beings.name: Satyrs
beings.summary: Half-goat nature spirits who follow Bacchus and embody wild desires. Roman satyrs have goat legs and human torsos, playing pipes and pursuing nymphs in wild forests. They represent the untamed sexual and creative energies that civilization tries to control.

beings.name: Camilla
beings.summary: Warrior maiden who could run across water without wetting her feet and fought Aeneas. Camilla was raised in the wilderness and became a swift warrior dedicated to Diana. She fought against Aeneas' Trojans but was killed by the Etruscan leader Arruns. Represents the Roman ideal of female martial virtue and the tragic cost of opposing destiny.

beings.name: Turnus
beings.summary: Rutulian king who opposed Aeneas' settlement in Italy and died in single combat. Turnus was the native Italian leader who resisted Aeneas' Trojan settlement, claiming the land for his people. He fought Aeneas in single combat for the right to rule, dying honorably but failing to prevent Roman foundation.

beings.name: Numa Pompilius
beings.summary: Second king of Rome who established religious and legal institutions through divine wisdom. Numa succeeded Romulus and established Rome's religious foundations, receiving laws from the nymph Egeria. He created the priestly colleges and legal system that governed Rome for centuries. Represents the Roman ideal of law-based civilization.

beings.name: Horatii Brothers
beings.summary: Triplets who fought Curiatii brothers to decide war between Rome and Alba Longa. Two Horatii died, but the survivor killed his sister for mourning the enemy, establishing Roman law against mourning enemies. Represents Roman values of state over family and the harshness of early Roman law.

beings.name: Cloelia
beings.summary: Roman maiden who escaped Etruscan captivity by swimming across the Tiber with hostages. Cloelia was given as hostage to the Etruscans but escaped by swimming the river while leading other Roman girls. The Etruscan king admired her courage so much he returned her. Represents Roman feminine virtue and courage in defense of the state.

beings.name: Lucretia
beings.summary: Roman matron whose rape by the king's son led to the overthrow of the monarchy. Lucretia was renowned for her virtue but was raped by Sextus Tarquinius, son of the last Roman king. Her suicide and her husband's vengeance led to overthrowing the monarchy and establishing the Republic. Represents the Roman ideal of feminine virtue as political force.

beings.name: Lucius Junius Brutus
beings.summary: Founder of the Roman Republic who led overthrow of the monarchy after Lucretia's death. Brutus was nephew of the last king but pretended stupidity to survive the tyrant's purges. After Lucretia's rape and suicide, he led the revolt that established the Republic. Represents the Roman ideal of republican virtue.

beings.name: Cicero
beings.summary: Great orator and philosopher who defended the Republic against Mark Antony. Cicero was the greatest Roman orator whose speeches saved the Republic from Catiline's conspiracy. He was eventually killed by Mark Antony for opposing Julius Caesar's dictatorship. Represents the Roman ideal of eloquence and republican virtue.

beings.name: Julius Caesar
beings.summary: Military genius who ended the Republic and became first Roman emperor through divine ambition. Caesar conquered Gaul, crossed the Rubicon, and defeated Pompey to become sole ruler. His assassination led to the end of the Republic and civil wars that established the Empire. Represents Roman ambition and the transition from Republic to Empire.

beings.name: Augustus
beings.summary: First Roman emperor who established the Pax Romana and imperial system. Augustus was Caesar's heir who defeated Mark Antony and Cleopatra, then established the imperial system that brought two centuries of peace. He created the imperial cult and administrative system. Represents the Roman ideal of ordered, peaceful empire.

stories.name: Aeneas Founding of Rome
stories.summary: Trojan hero fulfills divine destiny to establish Rome after wandering the Mediterranean.
stories.description: Aeneas escapes fallen Troy with his father and son, wandering under divine guidance through Carthage and Sicily before reaching Italy. He defeats Turnus and the native Italians, establishing the foundation that becomes Rome.

stories.name: Romulus and Remus Founding Rome
stories.summary: Twin sons of Mars disagree over city boundaries, leading to fratricide and foundation.
stories.description: Romulus and Remus, raised by a she-wolf, disagree on where to build their city. Remus mocks Romulus' walls by jumping over them, leading Romulus to kill him in rage. Rome is named after Romulus, founded in blood.

stories.name: Numa Establishes Roman Institutions
stories.summary: Second king receives divine wisdom to create Rome's religious and legal foundations.
stories.description: Numa Pompilius succeeds Romulus and establishes Rome's religious and legal systems. He receives wisdom from the nymph Egeria and creates priestly colleges and legal frameworks that govern Rome for centuries.

stories.name: Lucretia and the Republic's Founding
stories.summary: Virtuous matron's rape and suicide lead to overthrow of monarchy and Republic's birth.
stories.description: Lucretia, renowned for virtue, is raped by Sextus Tarquinius, son of the last king. Her suicide and her husband's vengeance lead to overthrowing the monarchy and establishing the Roman Republic under Brutus.

stories.name: Caesar Crosses the Rubicon
stories.summary: General crosses forbidden boundary, ending Republic and beginning imperial age.
stories.description: Julius Caesar, after conquering Gaul, crosses the Rubicon River with his legions, defying the Senate and beginning civil war. His victory leads to becoming dictator and ending the Republic, establishing the imperial system.

stories.name: Assassination of Julius Caesar
stories.summary: Emperor murdered by senators who fear his kingship, leading to civil wars and empire.
stories.description: Julius Caesar is assassinated by senators who fear his becoming king. His death leads to civil wars between his heir Augustus and his assassins, ultimately establishing the Roman Empire under Augustus.

stories.name: Augustus Establishes Pax Romana
stories.summary: First emperor defeats rivals and establishes two centuries of Roman peace and order.
stories.description: Augustus defeats Mark Antony and Cleopatra, becoming sole ruler of Rome. He establishes the imperial system and administrative framework that brings the Pax Romana - two centuries of peace and prosperity across the Mediterranean.

stories.name: Cloelia's Heroic Escape
stories.summary: Roman maiden escapes Etruscan captivity by swimming the Tiber with other hostages.
stories.description: Cloelia is given as hostage to the Etruscans but escapes by swimming across the Tiber River while leading other Roman girls. The Etruscan king admires her courage so much he returns her to Rome.

stories.name: Horatii Brothers Combat
stories.summary: Triplets fight to decide war between Rome and Alba Longa, establishing harsh Roman law.
stories.description: The Horatii brothers fight the Curiatii brothers to decide war between Rome and Alba Longa. Two Horatii die but the survivor wins, then kills his sister for mourning the enemy, establishing the Roman law against mourning enemies.

stories.name: Cicero Defeats Catiline Conspiracy
stories.summary: Great orator saves the Republic from conspiracy through eloquence and courage.
stories.description: Cicero discovers and defeats the Catiline conspiracy against the Republic through his powerful oratory. His speeches expose the plot and save Rome from overthrow, demonstrating the power of eloquence in defense of the state.

places.name: Troy
places.summary: Fallen city whose hero Aeneas escaped to found Rome, connecting Rome to heroic past.
places.description: Great city destroyed in the Trojan War, home of Aeneas before his flight to Italy. Troy represents the heroic past that Rome claims as its foundation, connecting Roman civilization to ancient heroic traditions.

places.name: Carthage
places.summary: North African city where Aeneas met Queen Dido during his wanderings.
places.description: Powerful city-state where Aeneas stopped during his Mediterranean wanderings. Queen Dido fell in love with him and offered him her kingdom, but he left to fulfill his destiny, leading to her tragic suicide.

places.name: Alba Longa
places.summary: Ancient city that warred with Rome before being absorbed into Roman territory.
places.description: Ancient Latin city that competed with early Rome for regional dominance. The Horatii-Curiatii combat decided war between the cities, with Rome eventually absorbing Alba Longa into its territory.

places.name: Rubicon River
places.summary: Boundary river that Caesar crossed, defying the Senate and ending the Republic.
places.description: River that formed the boundary between Caesar's province and Italy proper. His crossing with legions defied the Senate and began civil war, making 'crossing the Rubicon' synonymous with irreversible decisions.

places.name: Capitoline Hill
places.summary: Sacred hill where Rome was founded and became center of Roman religion and politics.
places.description: One of Rome's seven hills where Romulus founded the city and where the Capitoline Temple was built. It became the religious and political center of Rome, symbolizing Roman power and piety.

places.name: Tiber River
places.summary: River that flows through Rome, site of many founding and political events.
places.description: River that flows through Rome, where Romulus and Remus were found and where many key events occurred. Cloelia escaped across it, and it symbolizes the lifeblood of Rome and its connection to destiny.

places.name: Palatine Hill
places.summary: Hill where Romulus founded Rome and later emperors built their palaces.
places.description: Hill where Romulus originally founded Rome and where later emperors built their palaces. It became the center of imperial power and the word 'palace' derives from its name.

places.name: Senate House
places.summary: Political center of the Republic where senators debated and governed Rome.
places.description: Building where the Roman Senate met to debate laws and govern the Republic. It represented Roman political wisdom and the power of collective decision-making during the Republican period.

places.name: Roman Forum
places.summary: Public square where Romans conducted business, politics, and justice.
places.description: Central public space where Romans conducted business, political speeches, and legal proceedings. It represented the heart of Roman public life and civic participation in the Republic and Empire.

motifs.name: Divine Destiny in City Founding
motifs.summary: Cities are founded through divine will and prophetic fulfillment.
motifs.description: Pattern where cities, especially Rome, are founded through divine prophecy and destiny. Aeneas fulfills Jupiter's prophecy to found Rome, representing the Roman belief in divine sanction for their political authority.

motifs.name: Feminine Virtue as Political Force
motifs.summary: Women's virtue and suffering become catalysts for political change.
motifs.description: Pattern where women's virtue and their violation or suffering trigger major political changes. Lucretia's rape and suicide lead to the Republic's founding, representing the Roman belief that feminine virtue could shape politics.

motifs.name: Transition from Republic to Empire
motifs.summary: Political systems evolve from collective rule to imperial authority through crisis.
motifs.description: Pattern where political systems transform from Republican collective rule to imperial individual authority. Caesar's crossing of the Rubicon and assassination lead to Augustus establishing the Empire, representing political evolution through crisis.

motifs.name: Fraternal Conflict in City Founding
motifs.summary: City foundations often involve brotherly conflict and violent resolution.
motifs.description: Pattern where city foundations involve conflicts between brothers that end in violence. Romulus kills Remus over city boundaries, representing the Roman acceptance of violence in state-building and foundation costs.

motifs.name: Legal Precedents in Myth
motifs.summary: Mythical events establish legal principles that govern Roman society.
motifs.description: Pattern where mythological events create legal precedents that govern Roman law. The Horatii brother killing his sister for mourning enemies establishes the law against mourning enemies, representing myth's role in legal education.

motifs.name: Martial Virtue and Roman Citizenship
motifs.summary: Military courage and sacrifice establish the right to Roman citizenship and political power.
motifs.description: Pattern where military virtue and sacrifice establish claims to Roman citizenship and political authority. Heroes like Camilla and Cloelia demonstrate martial courage that earns Roman recognition, representing the military basis of Roman identity.

motifs.name: Imperial Peace and Administrative Order
motifs.summary: Empire brings peace through administrative systems and imperial authority.
motifs.description: Pattern where imperial systems bring peace through administrative order and centralized authority. Augustus establishes Pax Romana through administrative genius, representing the Roman belief in ordered empire as peaceful empire.

groups.name: Trojan Heroes
groups.summary: Survivors of fallen Troy who wandered the Mediterranean under Aeneas' leadership.

groups.name: Roman Senators
groups.summary: Political elite who governed the Republic and sometimes resisted imperial power.

groups.name: Imperial Family
groups.summary: Julio-Claudian dynasty that ruled Rome during the early imperial period.

groups.name: Vestal Virgins
groups.summary: Priestesses who maintained Rome's sacred fire and enforced sexual chastity.

groups.name: Augustan College
groups.summary: Religious college established by Augustus to promote imperial cult and traditional values.

objects.name: Shield of Aeneas
objects.summary: Divine shield depicting Roman future and Aeneas' divine destiny.

objects.name: Sibylline Books
objects.summary: Prophetic books purchased by Tarquin kings that guided Roman destiny and politics.

objects.name: Fasces
objects.summary: Bundle of rods with axe symbolizing Roman state authority and unity.

objects.name: Imperial Scepter
objects.summary: Symbol of imperial authority and divine right to rule the Roman Empire.

sources.title: Aeneid
sources.description: Epic poem telling the story of Aeneas and the founding of Rome, commissioned by Augustus.

sources.title: Livy
sources.description: History of Rome from its founding through the Republic, incorporating many myths and legends.

sources.title: Ovid
sources.description: Poet who wrote extensively about Roman mythology, including transformations and love stories.

sources.title: Plutarch
sources.description: Greek biographer who wrote parallel lives of famous Romans and Greeks, preserving many myths.

sources.title: Dio Cassius
sources.description: Roman historian who wrote about Roman emperors and incorporated many imperial myths and legends.

sources.title: Suetonius
sources.description: Biographer of Roman emperors who preserved many imperial myths and legends about early rulers.
